name: Tennessine
name_origin: Named after the U.S. state of Tennessee, recognizing Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University, and the University of Tennessee, which contributed the 249Bk target material.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna team led by Yuri Oganessian, with Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University, and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

occurrence.abundance_summary: Tennessine does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via the hot-fusion reaction 48Ca + 249Bk.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of tennessine has been experimentally characterized. Although it sits in group 17 below the halogens, relativistic calculations predict that strong spin-orbit splitting of the 7p shell will make tennessine behave more like a post-transition metal/metalloid than a classical halogen, with a likely stable +1 (and possibly +3) oxidation state and a less stable -1 state than chlorine, bromine, or iodine.
isotopes_summary: All tennessine isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotopes are 293Ts and 294Ts, both with half-lives on the order of tens of milliseconds. Their alpha-decay chains produce moscovium, nihonium, and lighter daughter nuclei.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Tennessine
Oganessian, Yu.Ts. et al. 'Synthesis of a New Element with Atomic Number Z=117', Phys. Rev. Lett., 2010
